MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Oct-05-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
David Wessel, Esq. (650.851.8880; service@efronlawfirm.com) of Law Offices of Boris E. Efron for Plaintiffs.
Michael Fox, Esq. (510.254.6750; mfox@krolaw.com) of Kronenberg Law PC for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is denied as defendant failed to maintain its initial burden of production.

An action in negligence requires a showing that the defendant owed the plaintiff a legal duty, that the defendant breached that duty, and that the breach was a proximate or legal cause of injuries suffered by the plaintiff. (See Ann M. v. Pacific Plaza Shopping Center (1993) 6 Cal.4th 666, 673.) The elements of a cause of action for premises liability are the same as those for negligence: duty, breach, causation, and damages. (Ortega v. Kmart Corp. (2001) 26 Cal.4th 1200, 1205; see also Castellon v. U.S. Bancorp (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 994, 998.) To demonstrate causation, a "plaintiff must show that the defendant's act or omission was a 'substantial factor' in bringing about the injury." (See Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763, 774.)

Here, Defendant fails to maintain its initial burden to show Plaintiff cannot establish causation. Defendant contends Plaintiffs cannot establish that Defendant's breach of a presumed duty was the legal cause of her injuries because she lacks personal knowledge of the causation. (Fox Decl. 2; Ex. A at 43:19-44:3.) Defendant submitted portions of Plaintiff Marina Gelman's deposition in support of their motion for summary judgment where Plaintiff Marina Gelman states she "cannot recall" "didn't pay attention" and failed to recall her toe catching or stubbing. (Fox Decl. 2; Ex. A at 43:19-44:3.) The Court rejects Defendant's argument that Plaintiff is unable to establish personal knowledge of causation. It is undisputed that Mr. and Mrs. Gelman are the only known witnesses to Plaintiff Marina Gelman's trip-and-fall. (Plaintiffs' Separate Statement in Opposition to Pacific Gas & Electric Company's Motion for Summary Judgment at #11.) Plaintiff Marina Gelman testified at her deposition that she knew where she fell and that she fell at the rectangular lid marked PG&E. (Fox Decl. 2; Ex. A at 52:1-6.) Plaintiff Marina Gelman's deposition testimony is not conclusory, but rather based on her recollection of relevant facts surrounding the trip-and-fall. In fact, if anyone is in the best position to know how she came to fall, Plaintiff Marina Gelman is. (See McGonnell v. Kaiser Gypsum Co., Inc. (2002) 98 Cal.App.4th 1098, 1104.) Moreover, Plaintiff Marina Gelman's declaration does not contradict her prior deposition testimony in which she stated multiple times that she recalled where she fell. (Fox Decl. 2; Ex. A at 52:1-6.) PG&E's D'Amico objection is overruled on the ground that it failed to elicit a "clear and unequivocal admission" in the deposition. Viewed in the light most favorable to Plaintiffs as the party opposing summary judgment, the trier of fact could conclude that Plaintiff Marina Gelman's injuries were caused by the defect. (See McCaskey v. California State Auto. Assn. (2010) 189 Cal. App.4th 947, 956-957; see also Stationers Corp. v. Dun & Bradstreet, Inc. (1965) 62 Cal.2d 412, 417.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
